content.problemStatement:
You own a delivery company in a city, which can be represented as a number line, starting at 000 and extending to +∞+\infty+∞.

In this city, there are NNN houses, where the iii-th house is located at district did_idi​. There is a postman, who can deliver to MMM houses (M≤NM \leq NM≤N).

The postman is bored and wants to have some fun. He first receives MMM indices, i1,i2,…,iMi_1, i_2, \ldots, i_Mi1​,i2​,…,iM​, of the houses assigned to him.

Following that, he gives these indices to his favorite LLM, asking it in what order he should make these deliveries.

Unfortunately for him, the LLM has malfunctioned! If he asks this question to the LLM, the LLM ends up always printing a random permutation PPP of length MMM, which describes the order in which he must make the deliveries.

After he receives the permutation, he goes from district diP1d_{i_{P_1}}diP1​​​ to district diP2d_{i_{P_2}}diP2​​​, then to district diP3d_{i_{P_3}}diP3​​​, so on, as described by the LLM.

Finally, he reaches district dipMd_{i_{p_M}}dipM​​​ and stays there for a good night's rest.

The distance the postman travels to move from district aaa to district bbb equals ∣a−b∣|a - b|∣a−b∣.

You, being the nice manager you are, feel bad for the postman, and know of his plan. Therefore, you decide to assign him houses so that the distance he is **expected** to travel due to the LLM is minimized.

Let this expected minimum value be ans\text{ans}ans. Furthermore, you are curious about how many ways you can assign houses to him, such that ans\text{ans}ans is still minimized.

Find ans×M\text{ans} \times Mans×M, and the number of different assignments. Since the number of assignments may be large, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

Note that it can be proved that ans×M\text{ans} \times Mans×M is always an integer.

Two assignments i1<i2<⋯<iMi_1 < i_2 < \cdots < i_Mi1​<i2​<⋯<iM​ and j1<j2<⋯<jMj_1 < j_2 < \cdots < j_Mj1​<j2​<⋯<jM​ are different if there exists an integer kkk such that ik≠jki_k \neq j_kik​=jk​.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the total number of houses, and the number of houses the postman can deliver to.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers d1,d2,…,dNd_1, d_2, \ldots, d_Nd1​,d2​,…,dN​ — the positions of the houses.




Output Format
For each test case, output two integers: ans×M\text{ans} \times Mans×M, and the number of assignments that minimize ans\text{ans}ans. The second number is to be printed modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 222: We can see that since M=1M = 1M=1, any assignment of cities will require him to not travel at all.
Therefore, all assignments minimize ans\text{ans}ans.
